title: O(n log n): Linearithmic
subtitle: The complexity of efficient sorting — and why

sections.heading: Where does this weird middle ground come from?
sections.body:
**O(n log n)** — sometimes called *linearithmic* — sits between linear and quadratic. It's the complexity of nearly every efficient sorting algorithm: merge sort, heap sort, and the average case of quick sort.

It appears whenever you do **n units of work** at each of **log n levels**. Think 'divide the problem in half, then merge them back together — and you have to touch every item to merge'.

sections.title: Merging two sorted decks of cards
sections.body:
You have two stacks, each already sorted. To make one sorted stack: look at the top of both stacks, take the smaller card, put it down. Repeat. To merge 100 + 100 cards takes 200 comparisons — linear in total size.

Now imagine starting from a shuffled deck. Split in half, sort each half (recursively), then merge. That's merge sort.

steps.text: Recursively sort each half. Splitting continues until each piece has 1 element.
Now merge pairs back together, in sorted order.

sections.heading: Deep dive: why O(n log n)?
sections.body:
Two things happen at each recursion:

1. **The number of levels is log n.** Each split halves the array. Halving n down to 1 takes log₂(n) levels — same logic as binary search.

2. **Each level does O(n) total work.** At the top level we merge two halves of total size n → n comparisons. At the next level we have 4 quarters of size n/4, with 2 merges of size n/2 each → still n total. At every level the total work across all merges is n.

Total: **(work per level) × (number of levels) = n × log n = O(n log n)**.

sections.caption: The merge-sort recursion tree — n work per level, log n levels

data.text:
[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]            ← level 0, n work
           ╱                              ╲
       [38, 27, 43, 3]              [9, 82, 10]     ← level 1, n work total
        ╱        ╲                    ╱       ╲
    [38, 27]   [43, 3]            [9, 82]    [10]   ← level 2, n work total
     ╱   ╲     ╱   ╲              ╱   ╲       │
   [38] [27] [43] [3]            [9] [82]   [10]    ← level 3, base cases

   Levels: log₂(7) ≈ 3
   Work per level: n
   Total work: O(n log n)

sections.heading: Trade-offs
sections.body:
Merge sort's strengths:

* **Guaranteed O(n log n)**, no bad case (unlike quicksort)
* **Stable** — equal elements keep their original order
* Easily parallelizable — each half can sort independently
* Great for linked lists (no random access needed)

Weaknesses:

* **O(n) extra space** — needs a scratch buffer for merging
* Constant factors are larger than quicksort in practice

Real-world JS engines often use **Timsort** (a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort) — see `Array.prototype.sort()`.

questions.question: Why is merge sort O(n log n) and not O(n²)?
options: Because log is faster than n
Because we do n work per level, but only log n levels
Because we never look at every element
questions.explanation: The recursion tree has log n depth, and each level merges a total of n elements.

questions.question: What's the space complexity of merge sort?
questions.explanation: Merging requires a scratch array of size up to n.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT O(n log n)?
questions.explanation: Bubble sort is O(n²) — it doesn't divide and conquer.

questions.question: If we sort n=10⁶ items, n log n is about ___ million operations.

sections.prompt: Write `mergeKArrays(arrs)` that merges k pre-sorted arrays into one sorted array. Example: `mergeKArrays([[1,4,7],[2,5,8],[3,6,9]])` → `[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]`. Aim for O(n log k) where n is the total element count.
hints: A naive 'merge them all into the first' is O(nk) — not what we want.
Pair up the arrays, merge each pair → k/2 arrays of double the size.
Repeat: k/4 arrays, then k/8, ... log k rounds total. Each round touches all n elements once. Total: n log k.
